proposals.old_exact: [¶8] in relevant part: A parent, adult family or household member, guardian, or other custodian of any child, who willfully commits any of the following offenses is guilty of a class C felony: 1. Fails to provide proper parental care or control, subsistence, education as required by law, or other care or control necessary for the child's physical, mental, or emotional health, or morals. N.D.C.C. § 14-09-22.1 (emphasis added).
proposals.new_exact: [¶8] Section 14-09-22.1, N.D.C.C. provides the offense of "neglect of child." It provides, in relevant part: A parent, adult family or household member, guardian, or other custodian of any child, who willfully commits any of the following offenses is guilty of a class C felony: 1. Fails to provide proper parental care or control, subsistence, education as required by law, or other care or control necessary for the child's physical, mental, or emotional health, or morals. N.D.C.C. § 14-09-22.1 (emphasis added).
proposals.evidence: The database text begins [¶8] with 'in relevant part:' but the authoritative PDF shows [¶8] should begin with 'Section 14-09-22.1, N.D.C.C. provides the offense of "neglect of child." It provides, in relevant part:'. This opening sentence is currently located at the end of [¶7], indicating an ingestion defect where a paragraph boundary was lost.

proposals.old_exact: [¶7] "Obvious error review consists of determining whether (1) there was an error, (2) that was plain, and (3) that affected a party's substantial rights." State v. Gaddie, 2022 ND 44, ¶ 4, 971 N.W.2d 811; see also State v. Olander, 1998 ND 50, ¶ 14, 575 N.W.2d 658. "An error is not obvious unless the defendant demonstrates it is a 'clear or obvious deviation from an applicable legal rule.'" Gaddie, at ¶ 4 (quoting Olander, at ¶ 15). When a defendant proves obvious error occurred, this Court has discretion "whether to rectify it and will only do so when the error 'seriously affects the fairness, integrity or public reputation of judicial proceedings.'" Id. (quoting Olander, at ¶ 16). Section 14-09-22.1, N.D.C.C. provides the offense of "neglect of child." It provides,
proposals.new_exact: [¶7] "Obvious error review consists of determining whether (1) there was an error, (2) that was plain, and (3) that affected a party's substantial rights." State v. Gaddie, 2022 ND 44, ¶ 4, 971 N.W.2d 811; see also State v. Olander, 1998 ND 50, ¶ 14, 575 N.W.2d 658. "An error is not obvious unless the defendant demonstrates it is a 'clear or obvious deviation from an applicable legal rule.'" Gaddie, at ¶ 4 (quoting Olander, at ¶ 15). When a defendant proves obvious error occurred, this Court has discretion "whether to rectify it and will only do so when the error 'seriously affects the fairness, integrity or public reputation of judicial proceedings.'" Id. (quoting Olander, at ¶ 16).
proposals.evidence: The introductory sentence of [¶8] ('Section 14-09-22.1, N.D.C.C. provides the offense of "neglect of child." It provides,') appears incorrectly at the end of [¶7] in the database. The source PDF clearly shows this text belongs to [¶8] as a separate paragraph. Removing it from [¶7] completes the structural correction.

proposals.old_exact: [¶8] Adams argues the manner in which he was charged with attempted gross sexual imposition resulted in inconsistent instructions to the jury and the charging of a non- cognizable offense. Adams concedes he failed to preserve this issue at trial but argues the asserted errors were obvious error requiring reversal. To establish obvious error, a defendant must show: (1) error; (2) that is plain; and (3) the error affects the defendant's substantial rights. State v. Pemberton, 2019 ND 157, ¶ 9, 930 N.W.2d 125.
proposals.new_exact: [¶8] Adams argues the manner in which he was charged with attempted gross sexual imposition resulted in inconsistent instructions to the jury and the charging of a non-cognizable offense. Adams concedes he failed to preserve this issue at trial but argues the asserted errors were obvious error requiring reversal. To establish obvious error, a defendant must show: (1) error; (2) that is plain; and (3) the error affects the defendant's substantial rights. State v. Pemberton, 2019 ND 157, ¶ 9, 930 N.W.2d 125.
proposals.source: PDF /Users/jerod/refs/nd/opin/pdfs/2024/2024ND139.pdf lines 108-113 show 'non-' at end of line 108 and 'cognizable' at start of line 109, indicating proper hyphenation across line break. DB incorrectly shows 'non- cognizable' with erroneous space before hyphen.
proposals.note: OCR ingestion artifact: space incorrectly inserted before hyphen when line break occurred at word boundary. The PDF shows correct 'non-' (with hyphen) at line break, DB shows 'non- ' (space before hyphen).

proposals.old_exact: [¶24] David McCay claims the district court erred in interpreting the fourth Stout- Hawkinson factor. The fourth Stout-Hawkinson factor considers: B The potential negative impact on the relationship between the noncustodial parent and the child, including whether there is a realistic opportunity for visitation which can provide an adequate basis for preserving and fostering the noncustodial parent's relationship with the child if relocation is allowed, and the likelihood that each parent will comply with such alternate visitation. Green, 2018 ND 258, ¶ 5.
proposals.new_exact: [¶24] David McCay claims the district court erred in interpreting the fourth Stout- Hawkinson factor. The fourth Stout-Hawkinson factor considers: The potential negative impact on the relationship between the noncustodial parent and the child, including whether there is a realistic opportunity for visitation which can provide an adequate basis for preserving and fostering the noncustodial parent's relationship with the child if relocation is allowed, and the likelihood that each parent will comply with such alternate visitation. Green, 2018 ND 258, ¶ 5.
proposals.evidence: PDF shows subsection marker 'B' on its own line 296, separate from the line containing the factor description. The DB incorrectly embeds the 'B' after the colon 'considers: B The' on line 94, when the 'B' should be on its own preceding line.
proposals.note: The 'B' is a subsection marker that should appear on its own line (as shown in PDF line 296) to denote the beginning of subsection B within section IV. Removing 'B ' (with space) from after the colon restores the proper paragraph flow.

coverage_note: Full opinion reviewed. The DB text (61 lines) is much shorter than the PDF text (141 lines) because pdftotext preserves line wrapping from the PDF, while the DB ingestion removed those. The PDF contains 4 page breaks (marked with 0x0C) and page numbers 1, 2, 3 which are formatting artifacts and not content. All substantive opinion text matches between DB and PDF except for the justices' names formatting issue identified. No OCR character/digit errors found, no missing text at page breaks, no citation errors."

Full opinion text verified against official PDF source using pdftotext extraction. Confirmed: All substantive content matches (sentence-level analysis, high-risk word verification, proper noun validation). All divergences are layout/formatting artifacts (line breaks, page numbers, page breaks) handled by separate passes per workflow rules. No OCR character confusion (0/O, l/1/I, rn/m, etc.) detected. Citation line breaks (14-/14.1 splits) are PDF extraction artifacts, not DB transcription errors.

flags.location_quote: Lines 6-20: 'Mariya Stephens, ... Plaintiff and Appellant ... v. ... Kevin Lee, ... and ... Defendant and Appellee ... State of North Dakota, ... Statutory Real Party in Interest'
flags.description: The caption party structure is incorrect. The DB has 'Kevin Lee,' on line 12, then 'and' on line 14, then 'Defendant and Appellee' on line 16, then 'State of North Dakota,' on line 18. This places 'and' between Kevin Lee's name and his designation. The PDF source shows the correct structure: 'Kevin Lee, Defendant and Appellee' followed by 'and' on a separate line, then 'State of North Dakota, Statutory Real Party in Interest'. This appears to be an OCR/ingestion artifact from how the multi-column centered PDF layout was processed.

proposals.old_exact: Ι
proposals.new_exact: I
proposals.source: PDF page 2 of 2024ND121.pdf - visual rendering shows proper Roman numeral 'I' (single Latin capital letter)
proposals.evidence: mutool rendering of PDF pages 2-5 shows section heading 'I' rendered as Latin capital I. Database contains Greek capital iota (U+0399) instead of Latin I (U+0049). This occurs at the start of section I of the Sargent opinion.

proposals.old_exact: Ш
proposals.new_exact: III
proposals.source: PDF page 4 of 2024ND121.pdf - visual rendering shows proper Roman numeral 'III' (three Latin capital I's)
proposals.evidence: mutool rendering of PDF page 4 shows section heading 'III' (three Latin I's). Database contains Cyrillic capital Sha (U+0428) instead of Latin III. This occurs at the start of section III in the Sargent opinion, between sections II and IV.

flags.location_quote: Lines 28, 42, 50, 60, 98, 128 — section headings: Ι, II, Ш, IV, V, VI
flags.description: Section heading sequence issue: first heading uses Greek iota (Ι = U+0399) and third heading uses Cyrillic Sha (Ш = U+0428) instead of Latin numerals I and III. This creates a sequence Ι, II, Ш, IV, V, VI instead of I, II, III, IV, V, VI. Both problematic characters are confirmed present once in database and are OCR-corruption errors where non-Latin lookalike characters substituted for proper Roman numerals.

flags.location_quote: V. Pagina Daanna Caadala Defendant and Appallant
flags.description: Extraneous corrupted caption line (DB line 5): This entire line contains multiple OCR errors: (1) 'V.' instead of 'v.' (uppercase vs. lowercase); (2) 'Pagina Daanna Caadala' is not a valid party name (likely corrupted OCR of something else); (3) 'Appallant' misspelling. This line appears to be a phantom duplicate of the correct defendant line that follows ('Regina Deanne Goodale, Defendant and Appellant' on line 6). The PDF shows only 'v.' on a separate line followed by the correct defendant name. This is a structural/caption integrity issue, not a simple character substitution. Should be reviewed for removal rather than character-level repair.

Dash character discrepancy in ¶24: DB uses em-dash (U+2014 '—') in 'eighty—five percent' but PDF shows en-dash (U+2013 '–') in 'eighty–five percent'. Both dashes are typographically acceptable and the text is fully legible. This is a minor OCR/normalization issue in dash-width selection. Low priority; cosmetic only.
